title: Chapter 20: The Short
content: The study smells like chemical sweetness — the fake-citrus tang of energy drinks cracked open and abandoned, their residue sticky on the glass coasters Jax never uses. I stand in the doorway and count them. Four cans. The fifth is in her hand, condensation bleeding down her wrist.
She doesn't look up.

The screens hum. All of them — the laptop, the two external monitors she must have carried in from the server room, the tablet propped against a stack of hardbound financial codes. Each one glows with a different shade of information: candlestick charts, SEC filing databases, a real-time feed of Hargrove Holdings' trading volume. The cold wallet sits on the desk beside her elbow, its tiny LED throwing a green pulse across the dark wood like a heartbeat counting down to something.

It's 6:47 in the morning. I know because I checked my phone three times on the drive here, watching the minutes tick past while the Pacific turned silver under a sky that couldn't decide if it was night or day. Jax hasn't slept. I can see it in the way her shoulders lock when she types, in the pink rimming her eyes, in the ferocious economy of her movements. No wasted keystrokes. No hesitation. Just the click-click-click of someone building something they've been building their whole life without knowing it.
"Jax."

She holds up one finger. Types. The charts on the left monitor shift — a red line trending downward in a simulation, then reversing, then plunging again. She's modeling scenarios. Stress-testing something. I step closer and catch a fragment on the tablet screen: SHORT INTEREST — HRGV — LEVERAGE RATIO 3.2:1 —
"You're shorting Hargrove Holdings."
Now she looks up. Her eyes are bloodshot but clear. Terrifyingly clear.
"Not shorting." She sets the energy drink down. The can clicks against the wood. "Constructing a position. There's a difference."
"Is there?"

"A short is a bet against. A position is a statement." She turns the laptop toward me, and I see it — the architecture of what she's been building since four in the morning, laid out in lines of logic so clean they might as well be poetry. Entry points. Exit strategies. Options chains layered like the floors of a building, each one calculated to maximize impact at the moment of exposure. The position is enormous. The margin requirement alone would make most hedge fund managers flinch.
"Jax, this is — "

"Forty-seven million in notional value," she says. "Leveraged through three shell entities I already control. The position builds in tranches so the market doesn't sniff it. By the time anyone notices the short interest, I'll have full exposure at the average price I want."

I pull a chair around and sit. The morning light is creeping through the glass wall behind her, turning the cliff side into a vertical canvas of shadow and gold. In an hour, the sun will hit the water and the whole room will blaze. Right now it's liminal — caught between the dark of what she's been doing alone and the light of what she's about to explain.
"Talk me through it," I say. "Like I'm not a finance person. Because I'm not."
Jax takes a breath. Then she starts, and her voice is the voice I've only heard a few times — the one that doesn't narrate or perform or deflect. The one that just tells the truth.

"Hargrove Holdings is publicly traded. The family controls forty-one percent of outstanding shares, but they leveraged that control against debt — real estate acquisitions, media expansions, defense contract fronting. Their entire power structure depends on the stock price maintaining a floor. The market believes in Hargrove Holdings because the Hargrove name means stability. Tradition. American fucking values." She says the last two words like they taste sour. "Tom's affair isn't just a personal scandal. It's a structural vulnerability. The moment it becomes public, the narrative breaks. Hargrove Holdings isn't a dynasty — it's a man who cheats on his wife with a woman he's gaslit into silence. The market doesn't price in moral failure, but it prices in instability. And nothing says instability like a CEO who's been lying to his board, his shareholders, and his wife simultaneously."
"So you short the stock, expose the affair, and — "

"And the stock collapses. The Hargroves lose their power base. Their debt-to-equity ratio inverts. Their credit lines freeze." She leans forward, and the green glow from the cold wallet throws shadows across her jaw. "Vivienne's marriage becomes a liability instead of an asset. Eleanor Sterling's leverage over Vivienne depends on Tom's financial standing — if Tom can't pay, the debt is uncollectable. You can't squeeze blood from a stone, Maya. But right now, Tom Hargrove isn't a stone. He's a golden calf. I'm going to turn him into rubble."
I stare at her. The screens hum. The energy drink cans line up like empty sentinels.
"This isn't revenge," I say slowly, because I need to hear her say it.

"No." The word is immediate and absolute. "This is a rescue operation. If I destroy Tom, I destroy Vivienne's world. She chose that world. I have to respect that even when I think it's a cage. But if I make the cage financially irrelevant — if Tom's power evaporates and the cage has no bars — then she's free to walk out without me pulling her. She doesn't leave because I forced her. She leaves because there's nothing keeping her."
The silence that follows is so complete I can hear the ocean beneath the cliff, the distant percussion of waves against rock.
"This is the most insane thing you've ever done," I say.
Jax smiles. It's a small, precise thing — the smile of someone who has thought through every objection before it's raised.
"This is the most sane thing I've ever done. Everything else was preparation."

Jordan arrives at nine. She comes through the front door without knocking — she's been doing that more lately, as if the formalities of visiting are something she's shed along with whatever loyalties she used to pretend. Her face is tight. She's carrying a messenger bag and a tension I can feel from across the room.

"You need to hear this," she says, and drops a folder on the kitchen island where Jax and I have migrated for coffee that neither of us needs. "Tom isn't just circling the wagons. He's on the offensive."

Jax opens the folder. Inside are printouts — emails, transcripts, a handwritten note on Hargrove Holdings letterhead. I lean over her shoulder and scan the contents, my stomach tightening with each line.

Tom has spoken to the Malibu zoning board. He's petitioned to revoke the Cliff House's special-use permit — the one that allows Jax to operate a home office in a residential zone. The petition cites "concerns about commercial activity inconsistent with neighborhood character." It's dated three days ago. The board meeting is in six days.

He's also called his contacts at the SEC. A formal inquiry into Jax's trading activities has been upgraded from preliminary to full investigation. They're looking at her positions from the last eighteen months, searching for patterns — insider trading, market manipulation, unregistered advisory activity. The kind of fishing expedition that doesn't need to catch anything to destroy your ability to operate.

And the FBI investigation — the one that's been circling since the beginning — Tom has fed it. He provided documentation. Testimony. He's positioned himself as a concerned citizen with knowledge of financial irregularities, which is rich for a man who's been hiding a mistress in a downtown apartment for two years.

"He's not defending his marriage," Jax says quietly. She's reading the SEC filing with the same expression she wore while building the short position — analytical, detached, almost appreciative of the craft. "He's executing a coordinated attack. Zoning, SEC, FBI — three vectors, all converging. He wants me out of Malibu. Out of the markets. Out."
"Out of the picture," Jordan finishes. "Permanently."

The coffee maker gurgles behind us. The morning sun has arrived exactly when I knew it would, turning the glass wall into a sheet of fire, and Jax stands in the center of it — backlit, still, a silhouette holding a folder full of evidence that a man with everything is trying to destroy a woman with nothing left to lose.

But that's the thing. She's not a woman with nothing left to lose. She has the short position. She has Mateo's information. And now she has confirmation that Tom is scared enough to mobilize every resource at his disposal.
"Good," Jax says, and closes the folder.
Jordan blinks. "Good?"

"He's overreaching. The zoning petition is thin — my permit has been clean for years. The SEC inquiry won't find anything because there's nothing to find; my positions are legal, documented, and mine. The FBI angle is his strongest play, but it depends on his credibility." She sets the folder down and looks at Jordan with those clear, sleepless eyes. "What does it look like when a man under personal scandal coordinates a government attack on the woman his wife is in love with?"
Jordan's mouth opens. Closes. She's a strategist. She sees it.

"It looks like retaliation," Jordan says slowly. "It looks like a man abusing his power to silence a romantic rival. The FBI case becomes tainted. The SEC inquiry becomes suspect. The zoning petition becomes personal harassment."
"Everything he's built to attack me becomes evidence against him." Jax picks up her phone. "The story isn't just the affair. The story is the cover-up. It's always the cover-up."
She's already dialing. I hear it ring twice before Mateo picks up.

"Mateo." Jax's voice shifts — not softer, exactly, but more deliberate. She's talking to someone she respects, and the respect is mutual enough that she doesn't need to perform competence. She can just be competent. "I need to talk to you about Cassandra."

She puts him on speaker. His voice fills the kitchen — low, cautious, carrying the weight of a man who has spent too many nights following someone else's secrets and not enough nights tending his own.
"I'm listening."

"The information you gave me about Tom and Cassandra — I need her cooperation. Not as leverage. As a witness. When this breaks — and it's going to break — she needs to be ready to tell her story. On her terms. In her words. With whatever protections she needs."
Silence on the line. The ocean fills it.

"And if she won't?" Mateo's voice is careful. I can hear what he's really asking: Will you make her? Will you do what powerful people do and turn a vulnerable woman into a weapon because it's convenient?
"Then I find another way," Jax says. "I won't force her."

She says it the way she said it to me about Vivienne. The same flat certainty. The same refusal to treat another person as a means to an end. I watch her face and I realize this is the axis of everything — not the short position, not the strategy, not the elegant financial weapon she's been constructing since four in the morning. This. The line she won't cross. The person she won't sacrifice.
Mateo is quiet for a long time. When he speaks again, there's something different in his voice — a shade of trust that wasn't there before.
"I'll ask her," he says. "No pressure. No timeline. I'll tell her what you're doing and why, and I'll let her decide."
"That's all I'm asking."

"Jax." Mateo's voice sharpens. "If she says yes — if she steps into this — you understand what that means for her. Tom won't just lose his stock price. He'll come after her. He'll come after everyone connected to her."
"I know."
"Then you'd better be ready to protect her."
"I will be."

The call ends. Jax sets the phone down and stares at the glass wall. The Pacific stretches to the horizon, impossibly blue, impossibly indifferent. Somewhere out there, Tom Hargrove is making calls of his own — zoning boards, SEC contacts, FBI handlers — convinced he's the one with the leverage. Convinced the cage he built for his wife is strong enough to hold.
He doesn't know the bars are already dissolving.

The afternoon light is different in the study — warmer, softer, turning the screens from hostile blue to something almost gentle. Jax has closed most of them. Only the laptop remains open, the short position sitting in its final configuration like a completed puzzle. The cold wallet's green LED still pulses. The energy drink cans have been cleared. The room smells like coffee now instead of chemical citrus, though I can still catch the ghost of it on the air.

Jax turns her chair to face me. There's something in her expression I haven't seen before — not vulnerability, exactly. Intentionality. The look of someone who has fought their way to clarity and is finally ready to act on it.
"I need you to document this," she says.
I wait.

"All of it. The short, the strategy, the truth about Tom and Cassandra. The FBI investigation. Jordan's intel. The zoning petition. Every piece. I need it written down and secured somewhere no one can erase it."
"Jax — "

"If I disappear — " She holds my gaze. "When I disappear, and the FBI is likely to make that happen at some point — the world needs to know what really happened here. Not the version Tom feeds to the press. Not the narrative the government constructs to justify raiding a woman's home and freezing her assets. The truth."
My throat tightens. "You're asking me to write your story."

"No." She leans forward. The green glow catches her eyes and for a moment she looks like something out of myth — a figure carved from code and conviction, sitting at the edge of a cliff, building a weapon out of numbers and light.
"I'm asking you to write OUR story."

The words hang in the air between us. Our story. Not Jax's brilliant short, not Vivienne's tragic marriage, not Tom's criminal cover-up. Ours. The story of a woman who built a financial empire and used it to set someone free. The story of a woman who refused to make weapons of the people she loved. The story of a woman who understood, somewhere in the space between four AM and morning light, that the most powerful position isn't long or short — it's the one where you stop playing the market's game and start writing your own.
"Okay," I say. "Okay. I'll write it."

Jax nods. She turns back to the laptop, and I see her fingers find the keyboard one more time — not typing, just resting. Feeling the architecture of what she's built. The position is set. The strategy is in motion. Everything from this point forward is execution.

But as I watch her sit there — silhouetted against the Pacific, the cold wallet's green pulse the only light in the room — I understand something she hasn't said yet. Something she might not even have articulated to herself.
The short position isn't the weapon.
The story is the weapon.
And I'm the one holding the pen.
